questions.question:
Consider the following two tables named Raider and Team in a relational database maintained by a Kabaddi league. The attribute ID in table Team references the primary key of the Raider table, ID.

Raider table:
ID | Name    | Raido | RaidPoints
1  | Arnav   | 250   | 219
2  | Ankush  | 190   | 219
3  | Mohan   | 150   | 200
4  | Rosa    | 150   | 190
5  | Prathan | 175   | 220
6  | Gopal   | 100   | 215

Team table:
City       | ID | BidPoints
Jaipur     | 1  | 200
Patna      | 3  | 195
Hyderabad  | 5  | 175
Patna      | 4  | 200
Jaipur     | 6  | 200

The following SQL query is executed on this database:
SELECT *
FROM Raider, Team
WHERE Raider.ID = Team.ID AND
      City = 'Jaipur' AND
      BidPoints >= 200;

The number of rows returned by the query is _______ (Answer in integer).
questions.to_find: Number of rows returned by the given SQL query

understand.plain: We have two tables: Raider (with player info) and Team (with city and bid details). The SQL query performs an inner join on ID, then filters for rows where the city is Jaipur and BidPoints is at least 200. We need to count how many rows satisfy all three conditions.

keywords.explain: Combining two tables by matching rows where a specified column has the same value in both tables.
keywords.example: Raider.ID = Team.ID links each raider to their team entry.

keywords.explain: After joining, only rows satisfying all conditions (City='Jaipur' AND BidPoints>=200) are kept.
keywords.example: ID=1 has City=Jaipur and BidPoints=200, so it passes.

understand.visual_alt: Table showing joined rows from Raider and Team; rows with ID=1 (Arnav, Jaipur, 200) and ID=6 (Gopal, Jaipur, 200) pass all conditions, giving 2 result rows.

given.aim: Count how many rows are returned after joining Raider and Team on ID, filtering for City='Jaipur' and BidPoints>=200.

terms.meaning: Inner join condition linking a raider to their team entry
terms.example: Raider(ID=1) joins with Team(ID=1, City=Jaipur, BidPoints=200)
terms.connects: Only 5 IDs appear in Team (1,3,4,5,6), so only those 5 raiders are in the joined result

terms.meaning: Filter retaining only rows where the Team city is Jaipur
terms.example: IDs 1 and 6 have City=Jaipur; IDs 3, 4, 5 have Patna or Hyderabad

terms.meaning: Filter retaining only rows with BidPoints of at least 200
terms.example: ID=1 has BidPoints=200 (passes), ID=6 has BidPoints=200 (passes)
terms.connects: Both Jaipur rows (ID=1 and ID=6) have BidPoints=200, so both survive

given.plan: First join the two tables on Raider.ID = Team.ID. Then apply both WHERE filters. Count the surviving rows.

steps.title: Perform equi-join on Raider.ID = Team.ID
steps.formula_raw: Result = Raider JOIN Team ON Raider.ID = Team.ID
steps.apply:
Team has IDs: 1, 3, 4, 5, 6. So the joined result has 5 rows:
(1, Arnav, 250, 219, Jaipur, 1, 200)
(3, Mohan, 150, 200, Patna, 3, 195)
(4, Rosa, 150, 190, Patna, 4, 200)
(5, Prathan, 175, 220, Hyderabad, 5, 175)
(6, Gopal, 100, 215, Jaipur, 6, 200)
steps.note: Raider ID=2 (Ankush) has no matching entry in Team, so it is excluded from the join result.

steps.title: Apply WHERE filter: City = 'Jaipur' AND BidPoints >= 200
steps.apply:
Check each joined row:
- ID=1: City=Jaipur ✓, BidPoints=200 >= 200 ✓ → included
- ID=3: City=Patna ✗ → excluded
- ID=4: City=Patna ✗ → excluded
- ID=5: City=Hyderabad ✗ → excluded
- ID=6: City=Jaipur ✓, BidPoints=200 >= 200 ✓ → included
Rows passing: 2
steps.note: Both Jaipur entries happen to have BidPoints exactly 200, which satisfies BidPoints >= 200.

solution.result: The number of rows returned by the query is 2.

questions.formula_note: Equi-join with WHERE filtering: join on equality of foreign key, then apply conjunctive predicates row by row.

questions.question:
An OTT company is maintaining a large disk-based relational database of different movies with the following schema:

Movie (ID, CustomerRating)
Genre (ID, Name)
Movie_Genre (MovieID, GenreID)

Consider the following SQL query on the relation database above:

SELECT *
FROM Movie, Genre, Movie_Genre
WHERE Movie.CustomerRating > 4 AND
      Movie_Genre.MovieID = Movie.ID AND
      Movie_Genre.GenreID = Genre.ID;

The SQL query can be sped up using which of the following indexing options?

A. B+ tree on all the attributes.
B. Hash index on Genre.Name and B+ tree on the remaining attributes.
C. Hash index on Movie.CustomerRating and B+ tree on the remaining attributes.
D. Hash index on all the attributes.
questions.answer: B. Hash index on Genre.Name and B+ tree on the remaining attributes.
questions.to_find: The best indexing strategy to speed up the given SQL query involving a range predicate and equality joins

understand.plain: We have a three-table join query. The WHERE clause includes one range condition (CustomerRating > 4) and two equality join conditions. We need to pick the indexing strategy that best supports these operations. B+ trees support both range and equality queries, while hash indexes only support equality. So any attribute involved in a range query must not use a hash index.

keywords.explain: A balanced tree index that supports both equality lookups (e.g., ID = 5) and range queries (e.g., Rating > 4) efficiently.
keywords.example: Finding all movies with CustomerRating > 4 uses a B+ tree scan from the key 4 onwards.

keywords.explain: An index based on a hash function; excellent for exact-match (equality) lookups but cannot support range queries.
keywords.example: Hash index on Genre.Name lets you find Genre rows with Name = 'Action' instantly, but cannot find genres starting with 'A'.

class='sub ink'>Equality (join)</text><text x='340' y='110' class='sub ok'>B+ Tree or Hash</text><rect x='10' y='120' width='480' height='28' class='bg' rx='2'/><text x='20' y='138' class='sub ink'>Genre.ID</text><text x='180' y='138' class='sub ink'>Equality (join)</text><text x='340' y='138' class='sub ok'>B+ Tree or Hash</text><rect x='10' y='148' width='480' height='28' class='hi' rx='2'/><text x='20' y='166' class='sub ink'>Genre.Name</text><text x='180' y='166' class='sub ink'>Equality only</text><text x='340' y='166' class='sub ok'>Hash OK</text><text x='20' y='192' class='sub mut'>Hash on CustomerRating is WRONG (range query needs B+ tree)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table showing each attribute, its condition type in the query, and the recommended index type. CustomerRating uses a range condition needing B+ tree; Genre.Name uses only equality so hash is fine.

given.aim: Determine which combination of B+ tree and hash indexes best accelerates the three-table join query with one range condition.

terms.meaning: A range predicate that selects movies with rating above 4
terms.example: Requires scanning a range of values; only B+ tree can efficiently support this
terms.connects: This eliminates hash index on CustomerRating (option C and D are wrong)

terms.meaning: Equality join between Movie_Genre and Movie tables
terms.example: Hash or B+ tree both work for equality joins
terms.connects: B+ tree on these IDs supports both the join and any future range queries

terms.meaning: An attribute in the Genre table; not part of the WHERE clause in this query
terms.example: No range predicate on Name, so a hash index would work fine if Name is ever used for equality lookup
terms.connects: Since no range query on Genre.Name, a hash index on it is acceptable; this is what option B proposes

given.plan: Check each WHERE condition type. Any attribute used in a range predicate must have a B+ tree. Attributes used only in equality conditions can have either B+ tree or hash. The correct option must not place a hash index on any range-query attribute.

steps.title: Identify range vs equality conditions in the query
steps.apply:
The query has:
1. Movie.CustomerRating > 4  →  RANGE predicate (requires B+ tree; hash index cannot be used)
2. Movie_Genre.MovieID = Movie.ID  →  EQUALITY join
3. Movie_Genre.GenreID = Genre.ID  →  EQUALITY join
Genre.Name does NOT appear in any WHERE condition in this query.
steps.note: Hash indexes work only for equality (=) lookups. For any > or < or >= or <= condition, only B+ tree can help.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply:
A. B+ tree on all attributes: Correct for functionality but not the 'optimal' choice — hash on equality-only attributes would be faster.
B. Hash index on Genre.Name + B+ tree on rest: Genre.Name has no range condition here, so hash is fine. B+ tree on CustomerRating handles the range. B+ tree on join IDs handles equality joins. This is a good choice.
C. Hash index on Movie.CustomerRating + B+ tree on rest: WRONG. CustomerRating > 4 is a range query; hash index cannot support range scans, so this would not speed up the query.
D. Hash index on all attributes: WRONG. Hash on CustomerRating cannot support CustomerRating > 4.
steps.note: Option B is the only one that correctly places a hash index only on an attribute with no range condition, while keeping B+ tree on the range attribute.

solution.result: Option B: Hash index on Genre.Name and B+ tree on the remaining attributes.

questions.formula_note: Index selection rule: B+ tree indexes support both range and equality queries; hash indexes only support equality. Any attribute used in a range predicate (>, <, >=, <=) must use a B+ tree index.
